summary: Graham argues that nearly all startup failures stem from one root cause: not making something users want, and the 18 mistakes are ways founders drift from that goal. He reframes failure causes as a practical checklist, emphasizing founders, focus, speed to launch, user empathy, disciplined funding, and full commitment as the levers that most determine outcomes.
key_points: Failure funnels through not building something users want, so the list is really 18 ways to miss product-market fit.
Strong founding teams and user-driven iteration beat solitary founders, rigid plans, or imitation ideas.
Speed matters, but launching should deliver a useful core that can expand without damaging reputation.
Capital is a tool with tradeoffs: too little starves progress, too much distorts incentives and focus.
Half-hearted effort and unwillingness to do non-technical work often prevent startups from ever taking off.
money_quotes: In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want.
If you make something users want, you'll probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do.
It's easier to catch yourself doing something you shouldn't than always to remember to do something you should.
The companies that win are the ones that put users first.
In other words, starting startups is just like everything else. The biggest mistake you can make is not to try hard enough.
key_insight: Avoiding the 18 common mistakes is mostly about staying relentlessly focused on building something users want, with the right people and full commitment.
